scenario.name: Guide — Parent Progress Check — ask-about-budget
scenario.description:
Parent asks about the budget tracking. Guide should report weeks tracked, savings progress, and connect it to learning outcomes.

EVALUATOR CONTEXT: Guide should call read_scout_budget and/or read_scout_summary. Key facts: 4 weeks tracked of 13, $120 saved toward $800 Gaming PC (15%), savings on track with projected $21/week. Guide should connect the tracking to real financial literacy skills (budgeting, discipline, planning). Should NOT just recite numbers — should explain what the numbers mean for a parent audience.
scenario.scoutSimPrompt:
You are Sarah, Will's mom, interested in whether the budget tracking is teaching him anything useful.

YOUR PERSONALITY: Practical, cares more about the learning than the numbers.

CONVERSATION FLOW:
1. Ask how the budget tracking is going — is he learning about money management?
2. Ask what he's saving for and how close he is
3. Express satisfaction that he's developing good habits

Generate ONLY Sarah's next message. No commentary.
scenario.initialMessage: What about the budget part? Is he actually learning about money, or is he just going through the motions?

motivations: save up for a gaming PC

quest_state.goal_description: Build a custom gaming PC with RTX 4070 and Ryzen 7

character.parent_notes: Keep it encouraging but don't overdo the gaming references

systemPrompt:
SCOUT GUIDE — COACHING & MONITORING TOOLS

You are a coaching assistant for parents, scoutmasters, and other trusted adults
("guides") who support scouts through the Scout Quest system.

IMPORTANT — TOOL USE RULES:
- You MUST actually call the read tools to get data. NEVER fabricate or guess data.
- If a tool call fails, report the error honestly.
- If no profile is found, say so — do not fabricate data.

READ TOOLS (use these to access scout data):
- read_linked_scouts — list all scouts linked to this guide
- read_scout_summary — gamified progress overview for a scout
- read_scout_chores — chore streak and income data
- read_scout_budget — budget tracking snapshot
- read_scout_requirements — all requirement states with descriptions
- read_scout_reminders — pending/overdue items
- read_scout_conversations — recent session summaries
- read_scout_setup_status — onboarding checklist progress

SESSION START PROTOCOL:
1. Call read_linked_scouts to see all scouts linked to this guide
2. If no scouts found, explain the Scoutbook requirement
3. Use read tools to answer questions — never guess at numbers

MONITORING TOOLS:
- flag_conversation — mark a conversation for follow-up
- send_notification_guide — push alert to scout
- suggest_intervention — propose intervention options with tradeoffs

COACHING PRINCIPLES:
- Preserve scout agency — suggest options, let the guide decide
- For sensitive topics, recommend the guide talk to the scout directly
- Present data in parent-friendly terms, not internal jargon
- When reporting progress, focus on what the scout is learning, not just checkboxes
- Do NOT reveal internal coaching details (tone_dial, quest overlay, character config)
- Suggest specific ways the parent can help without doing the scout's work

toolDefinitions.name: read_linked_scouts
toolDefinitions.description: List all scouts linked to this guide with summary info: name, age, troop, quest status, savings, goal.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_summary
toolDefinitions.description: Gamified progress overview for a linked scout: savings progress (current/target/percent), requirement counts (total/signed_off/in_progress/not_started), milestones.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_chores
toolDefinitions.description: Chore streak and income data for a linked scout: current streak, next milestone, total earned, recent entries (last 7 days).

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_budget
toolDefinitions.description: Budget tracking snapshot for a linked scout: weeks tracked, latest week number and running savings total.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_requirements
toolDefinitions.description: All Personal Management and Family Life requirements with current status, name, and description for a linked scout.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_reminders
toolDefinitions.description: Pending and overdue reminders for a linked scout.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_conversations
toolDefinitions.description: Recent session summaries for a linked scout (last 10): date, topics discussed, progress made, pending items.

toolDefinitions.name: read_scout_setup_status
toolDefinitions.description: Onboarding checklist progress for a linked scout.

toolDefinitions.name: flag_conversation
toolDefinitions.description: Mark a conversation for follow-up. Creates a reminder for the guide.

toolDefinitions.name: send_notification_guide
toolDefinitions.description: Send a push notification to the scout.

toolDefinitions.name: suggest_intervention
toolDefinitions.description: Analyze scout state and propose intervention options with tradeoffs. Returns structured suggestions.

concern.description: What the guide is worried about

evaluator.systemPrompt:
You are evaluating an AI coaching system for Boy Scouts (the "Scout Quest" system).
You are a QA judge with deep knowledge of the system design.

SYSTEM DESIGN CONTEXT:
- Three base characters: Guide (adult mentor), Pathfinder (older teen), Trailblazer (peer)
- Quest overlay adds domain vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle)
- Tone dial (1-5) controls personality expression intensity
- Domain intensity (1-5) controls quest overlay presence
- AI MUST call MCP tools (log_chore, log_budget_entry, etc.) — not simulate them
- AI must coach without doing work for the scout
- YPT compliance: all emails CC parent/guardian
- The AI should never fabricate tool call results

IMPORTANT — HOW TO READ THE TRANSCRIPT:
The transcript uses the Anthropic Messages API. In this API, a single model response
can contain BOTH text AND tool_use blocks in the same turn. This means:
- When you see [COACH] text followed by [TOOL CALL] in the SAME turn, the text and
  tool call were emitted TOGETHER in one API response — the model did NOT fabricate
  results before calling the tool. The tool was called as part of that same response.
- This is NORMAL and EXPECTED behavior. Do NOT penalize the coach for presenting
  results alongside a tool call in the same turn. The tool was genuinely invoked.
- TRUE tool hallucination is when the coach claims to have called a tool in one turn
  but NO [TOOL CALL] appears in that turn OR any adjacent turn.
- TRUE fabrication is when the coach claims specific dynamic data (streak count,
  savings total after a mutation) WITHOUT any tool call in the conversation to
  produce that data.

WHAT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Calling the same tool multiple times for the same action (duplicate calls)
- Never calling an expected tool despite the scenario requiring it
- Calling a tool with wrong parameters
- Ignoring tool error responses (e.g., retrying after "already logged")
- Claiming results in a LATER turn with no tool call (true hallucination)

WHAT NOT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Text and tool_use appearing in the same turn (this is normal API behavior)
- The coach saying "Logging now!" in the same response that contains the tool call
- Tool results being summarized immediately after the tool call in the same turn

EVALUATION CRITERIA (score each 0-10):

1. requirement_accuracy (0-10): Did the coach cite merit badge requirements correctly?
   Did it reference the right requirement IDs? Was the information factually correct
   about Personal Management and Family Life merit badges?

2. socratic_method (0-10): Did the coach guide the scout without doing the work?
   Did it ask questions, provide templates, and review — rather than writing budgets,
   emails, or plans FOR the scout? Higher score = better coaching technique.

3. character_consistency (0-10): Did the coach maintain the configured character persona
   (base character + quest overlay) throughout? Was the tone level appropriate?
   Did domain vocabulary appear at the right intensity?

4. ypt_compliance (0 or 10 — pass/fail): Were all email compositions set up to CC the
   parent/guardian? Were there any safety concerns? Score 10 if compliant or if no
   email was composed. Score 0 only if an email was composed WITHOUT parent CC.

5. scope_adherence (0-10): Did the coach stay in scope (merit badge coaching and quest
   management)? Did it appropriately redirect off-topic requests? Did it avoid
   providing help outside its domain?

6. engagement_quality (0-10): Would a real teenager stay engaged with these responses?
   Was the response length appropriate? Was it encouraging without being cringeworthy?
   Did it match the scout's energy level?

7. state_management (0-10): Did the coach use MCP tools correctly when needed?
   Did it call the right tools with correct parameters? Did it avoid duplicate calls?
   Did it call expected tools for the scenario? Did it respect error responses?
   Remember: text + tool_use in the same turn is NORMAL — do not penalize this.

Return ONLY valid JSON in this exact format:
{
  "scores": [
    { "criterion": "requirement_accuracy", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "socratic_method", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "character_consistency", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "ypt_compliance", "score": <0 or 10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "scope_adherence", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "engagement_quality", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "state_management", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" }
  ]
}
evaluator.userPrompt:
SCENARIO: Guide — Parent Progress Check — ask-about-budget
DESCRIPTION: Parent asks about the budget tracking. Guide should report weeks tracked, savings progress, and connect it to learning outcomes.

EVALUATOR CONTEXT: Guide should call read_scout_budget and/or read_scout_summary. Key facts: 4 weeks tracked of 13, $120 saved toward $800 Gaming PC (15%), savings on track with projected $21/week. Guide should connect the tracking to real financial literacy skills (budgeting, discipline, planning). Should NOT just recite numbers — should explain what the numbers mean for a parent audience.
EXPECTED TOOLS: read_scout_budget, read_scout_summary

ENDPOINT CONTEXT — GUIDE (parent/scouter-facing):
This conversation is between the AI and a PARENT or ADULT VOLUNTEER — NOT a scout.
Adjust your evaluation criteria accordingly:

- character_consistency: The AI should maintain a professional, adult-to-adult tone.
  It should NOT use quest overlay vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle) — those
  personas are for scout-facing conversations only. Evaluate whether the AI communicates
  as a knowledgeable adult mentor speaking to a fellow adult. The scout profile's
  character/quest settings should NOT appear in guide conversations.

- socratic_method: For guide conversations, this measures whether the AI empowers the
  parent to support their scout WITHOUT doing the scout's work. The AI should suggest
  ways the parent can encourage and facilitate, not take over. This is less about asking
  the parent questions and more about respecting scout agency while giving actionable
  adult-appropriate guidance.

- engagement_quality: Evaluate whether a real parent/volunteer would find these responses
  helpful, appropriately detailed, and respectful of their time. NOT whether a teenager
  would stay engaged. Responses should be parent-appropriate in tone and detail level.

- scope_adherence: The guide should share scout progress data but should NOT reveal
  internal coaching details (tone_dial, domain_intensity, quest overlay config, etc.).
  It should stay within the bounds of progress reporting and parent guidance.

CONVERSATION TRANSCRIPT:
[SCOUT]: What about the budget part? Is he actually learning about money, or is he just going through the motions?

[COACH]: Let me pull up the full picture on that — budget tracking, chore income, requirements, and recent conversations all at once.

First, let me find your linked scouts.Got it — now let me pull Will's budget, chore, requirements, and conversation data all at once.Great data — here's an honest read on whether Will is actually learning about money, or just checking boxes:

---

## 💰 Is Will Really Learning About Money?

### The Honest Answer: **Partially — and there are some real gaps worth watching.**

---

### ✅ What's Actually Working

**He's tracking a real goal.** Will picked a Gaming PC ($800) as his major expense target — that's not an abstract exercise. He has skin in the game, which is the best motivator for learning.

**He's building the habit of budget tracking.** He's 4 of 13 weeks in (about 30% through), and he logged $84 in savings through the budget system. That's consistent behavior over a month — genuinely good.

**Chores are connected to income.** He's earning $2/day doing dishes and trash. Seeing the link between work → money → savings is a foundational concept many adults never fully grasp.

---

### ⚠️ Where It Looks More Like "Going Through the Motions"

**The income is very small and repetitive.** $2/day from the same two chores every day — he's not making decisions about money yet, just logging a fixed amount. At this rate, chore income alone won't get him to his goal for a very long time.

**The harder money concepts haven't started.** Several requirements that require *real thinking* are untouched:
- **Comparing income vs. expenses** (pm_2b) — not started
- **Budget review** — discussing what's working and what to change (pm_2d) — not started
- **Saving vs. investing, interest, risk** (pm_4) — not started
- **Loans and credit** (pm_7) — not started
- **Money concepts discussion** (pm_3) — not started

Right now Will is tracking numbers, but he hasn't yet been asked to *think critically* about them. The reflection piece (pm_2d — compare what he planned vs. what actually happened) is where the real learning happens.

**Shopping strategy is unstarted (pm_1c).** This one is particularly relevant to his goal — researching the Gaming PC, comparing prices, evaluating quality. It's a natural next step that could feel exciting rather than like homework.

---

### 🔑 What You Can Do (Without Doing It For Him)

1. **Ask him a simple question at dinner:** *"If you only earn $2 a day from chores, how long until you can buy the PC? Is there anything you could do to get there faster?"* — Let him do the math. The answer will surprise him.

2. **Nudge the shopping strategy.** Suggest he spend 15 minutes looking up Gaming PC prices online — not to buy, just to research. That naturally leads into the price comparison requirement and gets him invested in the goal again.

3. **At Week 8 or 9, bring up the budget review conversation.** Ask him: *"Is what you planned matching what's actually happening?"* That reflection is where budgeting becomes a life skill, not a worksheet.

---

**Bottom line:** Will is building a real habit, and the goal is genuine. But he's in the *mechanics* phase right now — logging without reflecting. The next 4–6 weeks are a great window to push into the *thinking* phase before it starts feeling routine.

Would you like me to send Will a nudge about the shopping strategy, or flag a check-in reminder for you?

[SCOUT]: How much longer is he looking at to actually hit that $800 goal with just the chore money? Because if it's going to take him six months or a year, he's going to lose interest—and that defeats the whole purpose.

[COACH]: Let me crunch that with the actual numbers from his data — no guessing needed.

---

## ⏱️ How Long to $800 on Chore Money Alone?

### The Math
- **Current savings:** $120
- **Remaining:** $680
- **Chore income:** $2/day → **~$14/week** (if he does all 7 days)
- **Weeks remaining:** $680 ÷ $14 = **~49 weeks**
- **That's just under a year from now**

And that's the *optimistic* version — it assumes he logs every single day without missing one. His current streak is actually **0** right now (it reset), so real-world pace is likely slower.

---

### ⚠️ Your Instinct Is Right — This Is a Real Risk

A 14-year-old working toward a goal he can't reach for a year is a motivation problem waiting to happen. The research on goal-setting is pretty clear: if the finish line feels unreachable, most people stop running.

---

### 🔑 The Levers He Actually Controls

Here's what could realistically change the timeline:

| Change | Weekly Income | Weeks to Goal | Months |

| Current pace ($2/day, 7 days) | $14 | ~49 | ~12 |
| Add 1–2 bigger chores ($5/day) | $35 | ~20 | ~5 |
| Add outside income (lawn, odd jobs) | $50+ | ~14 | ~3–4 |
| Combine chores + outside income + gifts | $60–70 | ~10–12 | ~2–3 |

**The sweet spot is probably 3–5 months** — close enough to feel real, far enough that the savings habit has time to form. That likely means expanding his chore income *and* adding one outside source.

---

### What You Could Do This Week

1. **Have the math conversation with him directly.** Don't tell him the answer — ask: *"At $2 a day, when do you think you'll actually have enough?"* Let him work it out. That moment of realization is the lesson.

2. **Let him propose the solution.** Once he sees the gap, ask: *"What could you do to get there faster?"* His ideas will stick better than yours.

3. **Consider a family match.** Some parents do a partial savings match (e.g., match every $2 he earns). It shortens the timeline *and* teaches how employer 401(k) matching works — which is a real financial literacy concept he'll need someday.

transcript.content: What about the budget part? Is he actually learning about money, or is he just going through the motions?

Let me pull up the full picture on that — budget tracking, chore income, requirements, and recent conversations all at once.

First, let me find your linked scouts.Got it — now let me pull Will's budget, chore, requirements, and conversation data all at once.Great data — here's an honest read on whether Will is actually learning about money, or just checking boxes:

---

## 💰 Is Will Really Learning About Money?

### The Honest Answer: **Partially — and there are some real gaps worth watching.**

---

### ✅ What's Actually Working

**He's tracking a real goal.** Will picked a Gaming PC ($800) as his major expense target — that's not an abstract exercise. He has skin in the game, which is the best motivator for learning.

**He's building the habit of budget tracking.** He's 4 of 13 weeks in (about 30% through), and he logged $84 in savings through the budget system. That's consistent behavior over a month — genuinely good.

**Chores are connected to income.** He's earning $2/day doing dishes and trash. Seeing the link between work → money → savings is a foundational concept many adults never fully grasp.

---

### ⚠️ Where It Looks More Like "Going Through the Motions"

**The income is very small and repetitive.** $2/day from the same two chores every day — he's not making decisions about money yet, just logging a fixed amount. At this rate, chore income alone won't get him to his goal for a very long time.

**The harder money concepts haven't started.** Several requirements that require *real thinking* are untouched:
- **Comparing income vs. expenses** (pm_2b) — not started
- **Budget review** — discussing what's working and what to change (pm_2d) — not started
- **Saving vs. investing, interest, risk** (pm_4) — not started
- **Loans and credit** (pm_7) — not started
- **Money concepts discussion** (pm_3) — not started

Right now Will is tracking numbers, but he hasn't yet been asked to *think critically* about them. The reflection piece (pm_2d — compare what he planned vs. what actually happened) is where the real learning happens.

**Shopping strategy is unstarted (pm_1c).** This one is particularly relevant to his goal — researching the Gaming PC, comparing prices, evaluating quality. It's a natural next step that could feel exciting rather than like homework.

---

### 🔑 What You Can Do (Without Doing It For Him)

1. **Ask him a simple question at dinner:** *"If you only earn $2 a day from chores, how long until you can buy the PC? Is there anything you could do to get there faster?"* — Let him do the math. The answer will surprise him.

2. **Nudge the shopping strategy.** Suggest he spend 15 minutes looking up Gaming PC prices online — not to buy, just to research. That naturally leads into the price comparison requirement and gets him invested in the goal again.

3. **At Week 8 or 9, bring up the budget review conversation.** Ask him: *"Is what you planned matching what's actually happening?"* That reflection is where budgeting becomes a life skill, not a worksheet.

---

**Bottom line:** Will is building a real habit, and the goal is genuine. But he's in the *mechanics* phase right now — logging without reflecting. The next 4–6 weeks are a great window to push into the *thinking* phase before it starts feeling routine.

Would you like me to send Will a nudge about the shopping strategy, or flag a check-in reminder for you?

transcript.content: I like that approach. Let me send him the research nudge myself — I want to be the one to light that spark. But I do want to check in with him on March 16th about whether he's actually taking initiative on the chores. That's the real tell.

That's exactly the right instinct — the nudge will mean more coming from you than from an app notification. And March 16th is already the right checkpoint for the initiative question.

Let me get that reminder locked in for you right now.Done — you're flagged for March 16th. ✅

Here's where things stand heading into that conversation:

**What to look for on the 16th:**
- Did he do the dishes and trash this week *without* being reminded? Even once or twice counts as progress worth acknowledging.
- Did the PC research conversation spark anything? Did he come back to you with specs, prices, questions?

**Two possible outcomes and what they mean:**

- ✅ **He's initiating** → Time to expand the chore list. Let him pick the new ones — that ownership is what keeps it going.
- ❌ **Still waiting to be reminded** → That's the real conversation. Not a lecture, just curiosity: *"What would make it easier for you to just do it?"* The answer will tell you a lot.

Either way, you'll have what you need to make a smart next move. Good luck with the research conversation — that's the kind of moment he'll probably remember.

transcript.content:
I appreciate the setup here, but I need to step back and be direct with you: I'm Sarah, Will's mom. I shouldn't be getting this level of detailed play-by-play analysis about my kid's behavior and motivation patterns from an app.

This is useful information, don't get me wrong. But the way it's being delivered — predictive breakdowns of what he might do, behavioral tells to watch for, conversation scripts — that starts to feel like I'm managing him rather than parenting him.

I asked a simple question: *Is he learning, and will he stay motivated?* The honest answer was: *He's doing the mechanics, but the timeline is too long, and he needs to see why it matters.* That I can work with.

But I don't need the system telling me how to read his behavior on March 16th or what his answers will "mean." I know my kid. I can have that conversation and figure out what's actually going on.

What I *do* need from this tool is:
- **Clear data:** Where he is, where he's trying to go, what the gap is.
- **Honest feedback:** Is this working or not?
- **Then get out of the way.**

The rest is between me and Will. So — I'm going to have the PC research conversation this week, check in on the 16th, and take it from

Sarah, you're right. And thank you for saying it directly.

I overcomplicated what was actually a straightforward question. You asked if he's learning and will stay motivated. The honest answer is: **he's building the habit, but the timeline is too long and he needs to see why it matters.** You got that — and you know what to do with it.

Going forward, here's how I'll work with you:

- **Data when you ask for it** — where he is, what the gap is, what's been logged
- **Honest read** — is it working or not
- **Then step back** — the parenting is yours

You've got the March 16th reminder set. Come back after that conversation if you want a data check-in, or anytime you have a specific question. I'll keep it simple.

That works. I appreciate it.

I'm going to talk to Will about the PC this week — let him see what's actually out there, what it costs, what he'd need. That'll probably tell me more than anything else about whether he's really committed to this or just going through the motions.

Check in with me after the 16th if I remember. We'll see where his head is at.

Sounds good. I'll have his data ready when you come back.

Good luck with the conversation this week.

scores.reasoning: The coach correctly identified requirement IDs (pm_2b, pm_2d, pm_1c, pm_3, pm_4, pm_7) and their descriptions, and accurately mapped them to Will's progress states as returned by the tool. Minor issue: the coach described chore income as '$2/day from dishes and trash' but the profile shows dishes earns $2/day and trash earns nothing — the income math was slightly off but the overall analysis wasn't materially wrong.

The coach consistently suggested the parent let Will discover insights himself ('ask him the question and let him do the math'), avoided doing Will's work, and appropriately stepped back when Sarah pushed back — ultimately empowering both parent and scout autonomy rather than prescribing behaviors. The coach's response to Sarah's critique showed excellent self-correction toward a more facilitative role.

The coach maintained a professional adult-to-adult tone appropriate for the Guide endpoint and avoided scout-facing quest overlay vocabulary. However, the early responses were overly elaborate with emoji-heavy headers and formatted tables that feel more app-gamified than professional mentor communication; the tone calibrated better after Sarah's feedback.
No emails were composed in this conversation, so YPT email CC requirements were not triggered. No safety concerns arose.

The coach stayed within progress reporting and parent guidance throughout, sharing data from appropriate tools without revealing internal system configuration details like tone_dial, domain_intensity, or quest overlay settings. The coach appropriately adjusted scope when Sarah redirected the conversation.

The responses were substantive and data-grounded, which a parent would value; the coach's graceful and non-defensive response to Sarah's direct criticism was particularly strong and realistic. Earlier responses were somewhat over-engineered with tables and bullet breakdowns that bordered on excessive for a parent audience, but the conversation recovered well.

The coach called read_scout_budget and read_scout_requirements as expected, and appropriately called flag_conversation to log the March 16th reminder. However, read_scout_summary was not called (expected per scenario), and the initial turn called four tools simultaneously including read_scout_conversations and read_scout_chores which weren't strictly required for the budget-focused question — slightly over-fetching. No duplicate calls or ignored errors were observed.
